text: Since there is nothing happening, time for some revision. It's all about the refined products. #oott

quotedTweet.text:
Refinery 101 (and maybe a bit more)

Refining is complicated. Put in crude A in refinery B will give you different products than putting crude A in refinery C. 

Each unit in the refinery is unique and produces different products. Later you blend them back together to make a final product (like gasoline and fuel oil). What a strange system but that's how it works.

I have tried to simplify the main units to as simple terms as I can in the attached refinery configuration schematic. For those in the industry, you will say that's too simple. But those who are oil tourists, this is going to look like rocket science. 

Remember I talked about the min 60% requirement in the refinery? That limit applies to every single unit highlighted in the configuration schematic, not just the CDU. And there are countless more units that I have NOT shown in the chart (e.g. hydrotreaters, desalters, alkylation, etc).

Regardless, I do hope this helps explains some of the stuff that I have been talking about in the past few weeks. 

This week's @SpartaCommo podcast in particular talks about the way fuel oil gets upgraded and the hard choices the refinery has to make when you have less VGO and short residue available. 

Stay tuned. The stress is still in refined products and will be for some time.

#oott

text:
Taking stock 2 months in: Refined products E/W drop

1⃣The Eastern product prices have come off relative to Western prices across the board in April vs March.

2⃣This is due to refinery intakes running slightly higher in April than in March as arb and SPR crudes make their way into the refining feedstock. 

3⃣Initial panic buying of products (mogas, diesel and jet fuel in particular) led to a more inventory in Asia then required by 2H April. 

4⃣With backwardation high, sellers do not want to be caught at the end month carrying stocks and are actively offering to a market that is now working more on a hand-to-mouth basis.

5⃣Europe is now catching a whiff of product supply shortages, thus strengthening the West side pricing. Europe however has the benefit of diesel and mogas IEA SPR to draw on, unlike Asia which is predominantly crude SPR.

6⃣However we think Asia refining runrates are going to remain low into May and with a lighter diet as well, leading to significant loss in middle distillates and fuel oil yields. Diesel and 0.5% E/W thus has significant upside in our view.

7⃣Warning bells on petchem remains high, with the latest news that Vietnam's Long Son Petrochemical will suspend operation in mid May due to naphtha availability. They are now planning to accelerate their diversification project to include US ethane as feedstock that was originally planned for end 2027.

#oott

on the difficulties faced by Singapore's refineries in diversify from Middle East crude.

It's not optimal, but at least we can still run to produce key refined products for now.

#oott

https://t.co/vOnhjTd6t1
I don't know why I keep having typos. In diversifying from Middle East crude. Sorry folks!

The second largest Chinese independent refinery Hengli was sanctioned by OFAC today. China's teapots with access to sanctioned barrels have actually been running harder compared to falling runrates at state owned refiners who have access to SPR but not really tapped on it. 

These teapots only serve the domestic market. It's a message from China's government to run mainly for internal consumption. Refined products from the state owned enterprises are technically banned and we see that particularly reflected on decreased mogas exports.

Is this going to stop Iranian flows into China? Doubt it. 

For a holistic update of the rest of the oil markets in the past week, do tune in to our latest podcast!

#oott

text: The Story of Mr Crude here from 1 month back: https://t.co/tSSjVeeVUb

quotedTweet.text:
The Story of Mr Crude

I start my journey as a fossil fuel all the way in the Middle East (probably the dinosaur was my ancestor). I get loaded onto a ship called a Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) and get discharged into somewhere in Asia, with so much refining capacity but limited availability of domestic crude.

Then in the refinery, I get distilled (i.e. split up) and upgraded into various fractions. The light ends: LPG, naphtha, mogas. The middle distillates: jet fuel, diesel. The bottom of the barrel: fuel oil.

All these little fractions are called refined products. If you don't have little old me (i.e. Mr Crude), and if the refineries are not having enough to run, there will be no refined products.

What's the big deal then? Are you on your phone or computer reading this post? In a room with air conditioning? Almost everything you see has links with me, whether the fuel to generate electricity (in some countries), or the material to make the phone and computer (plastics, from naphtha), to the delivery of goods to your home (transport fuel).

So if 20% of the world's oil supply is blocked for more than 3 weeks, I think we have a problem, sir.

Yours sincerely,
Mr Crude

#oott

Global oil demand is always harder to track than global oil supply. In order to track demand, you use refinery runs, check product storage, imports/exports, and then back in an implied demand figure. 

Since refineries are now cutting runs, the base assumption is that this is reduced demand. But that’s not entirely the case. There are only four regions that report weekly oil inventories. 

Europe, US, Fujairah, and Japan. Well, Japan stopped, so it’s only 3 of them now. 

On the satellite side, you can see floating storage tanks. So most service providers can only give you crude data and not products. 

What am I trying to say?

It’s hard to know the impact on demand. The best and most efficient way has been to use refining margins as a proxy. Everything is subject to data fluctuation. That has been the most effective in my experience. 

So there is demand loss now, but I don’t know if it’s 1.5 or 4.5 million b/d. Oil specialists are already at the point of not even bothering with the differences of a few million barrels because the supply outage is too large to matter. 

It is what it is.

This is comical. 

Everyday before the market opens, Pakistani sources are at it again. 

Meanwhile, Strait of Hormuz is still closed and we are losing 7.5 million bbls per day in global oil inventories.

quotedTweet.text:
Overnight, pursuant to a seizure warrant submitted by my office and signed by a federal magistrate, U.S. forces interdicted the dark fleet vessel, M/T Majestic (formerly known as the M/T Phonix), while carrying approximately 1.9 million barrels of Iranian oil in the Indian Ocean. Earlier this week, U.S. forces similarly seized the M/T Tifani based on one of our seizure warrants. It too was laden with approximately 1.9 million barrels of Iranian oil.  
 
We will continue to relentlessly investigate, track, and pursue these cases, using every lawful authority to hold sanctioned actors and those who support terrorism accountable, and to deny them of any ability to profit from illicit maritime activity.
 
My office has executed some of the nation’s most complex seizure warrants—including those used to stop, board, and seize the M/Ts Skipper, Bella 1, and Veronica—working with national security partners to disrupt illicit oil networks and protect American interests.

Nickel is staring down a massive multi-year oversupply, and the market indicators look like trash right now.

But Indonesia, which handles half the global output, seems ready to pull the trigger on production cuts. According to APNI, the local nickel association, Indonesia’s output could drop to around 250M tons in 2026—a huge haircut from this year's roughly 379M tons.

Plus, if a mining giant like Lundin is working to position Talon as the only nickel-copper play in the US with real scaling potential...

There might be an opening for nickel after all. It won't wipe out all the glut, but even a slight shift away from peak bearish consensus could send prices flying.

#nickel

text:
Here we go again x 100

Have a good weekend.

Those playing 4D chess hope that a suffering China will eventually pressure Iran into a forced peace. 

What they fail to grasp is that China lacks total control over Iran. The relationship between China and Iran is nothing like that of South Korea or Japan with the US. 

It is not a subordinate relationship where one acts as the other's lapdog. Iran is not China's lapdog. Furthermore, China will be the last to feel the pain. 

The world is on the brink of the fastest onshore crude inventory drawdown in history. Amidst this, China will hold up quite well, thanks to the buffer they have wisely built up.

China will eventually feel the heat from this turmoil, but they will certainly be the last ones to get burned.

COT on #commodities covering the week to 21 April saw relatively muted activity from the managed money category.
Following weeks of upheaval across the energy sector, the Bloomberg Commodity Index Energy Subindex posted a modest 2.5% weekly gain, resulting in only limited positioning changes across WTI, Brent, and refined fuel product futures.
Metals were mixed, with renewed weakness in precious metals triggering broad net selling, while copper attracted buying for a third consecutive week, lifting the net long further.
In agriculture, buying continued across the three major crops, led by soybeans and corn. Softs were mixed, with a one-third reduction in the sugar long and a doubling of the cotton long drawing the most attention, while all three major livestock contracts saw net selling. 
More in my Monday update.

The latest COT on #FX report, covering the week to 21 April, captured a wait-and-see period as traders and investors looked for fresh signals from the Middle East, where a ceasefire held while both Iran and the US maintained maritime blockades that continued to severely restrict flows of energy, metals, and other key commodities from the Persian Gulf.

While the dollar traded modestly firmer, FX speculators sold the USD for a second consecutive week. Overall, the gross dollar long against eight IMM futures contracts was cut by 20% to USD 11.7 billion, down from a 14-month high at the beginning of April.

Positioning flows were mixed but overall tilted towards renewed dollar selling, led by a second week of strong EUR demand, short covering in CAD, and fresh longs in MXN. The most notable selling, however, was seen in JPY, where renewed bearish bets lifted the net short to a 21-month high.

Global #copper stockpiles monitored across the three major futures exchanges fell for a fifth consecutive week, declining by 36 kt to 1,144 kt. The drawdown continues to be driven by a rapid reduction in China, where SHFE inventories have more than halved over the period to 201 kt, the lowest since January. Elsewhere, the LME posted its first weekly decline in 3½ months, easing to 393 kt, while COMEX inventories rose for a second straight week to 550 kt.
Futures in NY and London ended the week near with small losses after failing to break resistance around $13,525/t and $6.15/lb.
Cheers Pip.  Have a good one
#Commodities weekly: From fuel shortages to food risks as Hormuz remains shut https://t.co/0oyxcdPLWi via @saxobank

The #crudeoil market is increasingly "watching the ball, not the players." as traders and investors have shifted their focus away from diplomatic rhetoric and toward tangible action on the water. To date, besides the prolonged ceasefire offering some hopes, there have been no action capable of cooling the upward pressure on prices. Aside from involuntary demand destruction and reduced refinery run rates due to surging input costs, the fundamental supply-side tightness remains unaddressed. In this environment, the market has to focus on what is actually happening on the water, rather than what is being promised or said in a random social media post.

#Commodities update:

As the Middle East crisis continues, and with it the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the Bloomberg Commodity Index is heading for a weekly gain of more than 3%, lifting its year-to-date return above 25%. The advance has been supported by renewed strength across the energy sector, where all components except US natural gas are higher, delivering an 11% gain. Together with broad, albeit modest, gains across agriculture, this has more than offset a near 5% loss in precious metals, while industrial metals have traded mixed. Fuel products have led the advance, not least gas oil and diesel, followed by soybean oil, wheat and coffee. Gold, silver and natural gas have been the biggest drags on overall performance.

US natural gas futures tumbled after a six-day winning streak, with the May contract slumping to $2.57 after the EIA reported a bigger-than-expected 103 bcf weekly injection into domestic stockpiles. Inventories rose to 2,063 bcf, leaving them 7.1% above the five-year average. The stock build reinforced concerns about oversupply, driven by mild seasonal weather and soft demand, even as drillers have started curbing activity in response to weaker prices.

Oil trades higher for a fifth day, with no apparent end in sight to the Middle East stalemate as the US and Iran continue to block access through the Strait of Hormuz. The disruption threatens to further delay flows of crude, fuel, chemicals, metals and fertilizers from the Persian Gulf. Even a full reopening may still leave flows taking several months to normalize, creating additional tightness, especially in diesel and jet fuel, and forcing countries and companies to curb demand. Meanwhile, US shale executives appear in no hurry to increase production despite higher prices, given heightened uncertainty over the outcome of the war and current market volatility.

Agriculture: adding to the current fertilizer shortage, which has already raised concerns about global crop yields, NOAA confirms that ENSO-neutral conditions are expected to persist through June 2026 before transitioning to an El Niño from July with a +60% probability - raising the risk of droughts and floods in key producing regions, depending on location.

text:
US #natgas prices tumbled after a six-day winning streak, with the May contract slumping to $2.58 after the EIA reported a bigger-than-expected 103 bcf weekly injection into domestic stockpiles. Inventories rose to 2,063 bcf, leaving them 7.1% above the five-year average.

The stock build reinforced concerns about oversupply, driven by mild seasonal weather and soft demand, even as drillers have started curbing activity in response to weaker prices.

🚨Goldman Sachs: How Fast Can Gulf Production Recover After Reopening? (April 23, 2026 report)

This detailed note lands right in the middle of the massive supply shock from the Hormuz closure and directly reinforces the core thesis of my Forbes piece last month: the oil price narrative has decisively shifted from “lower for longer” to higher for longer.

Even under Goldman’s relatively optimistic base-case assumptions (safe, full reopening of the Strait with no renewed strikes on oil assets), the recovery is far from instantaneous, and the risks of delays or incomplete ramp-ups keep the market structurally tight for months.

Here are the key points:

• Persian Gulf crude output has been slashed by 14.5 million barrels per day, a staggering 57% drop from pre-conflict levels. Most of this is precautionary shut-ins and inventory management rather than widespread physical destruction of fields (unlike LNG facilities).

• Best-case recovery: Mostly back within a few months once the Strait reopens safely. External forecasts (EIA/IEA/Rystad average) see ~70% of lost output restored after 3 months and ~88% after 6 months.

• Saudi Arabia and the UAE can potentially deploy over 2 million bpd of spare capacity quickly to help stabilize global balances.

• Major headwinds remain: tanker availability down by 50% (empty capacity fell by 130 million barrels), reservoir pressure/flow-rate issues (especially in lower-pressure fields in Iran and Iraq) requiring extensive workovers, procurement delays for materials and crews, and destocking bottlenecks.

• The longer the shutdown lasts, the greater the risk of permanent reservoir “scarring,” slower final recovery, or even partial non-recovery.

Bottom line: Yes, reopening brings relief — but not overnight cheap oil. The combination of logistical friction, potential scarring, and ongoing geopolitical overhang means elevated prices and tight balances will persist well into 2026 and likely 2027. 

Exactly as I argued in Forbes: the era of expecting a quick return to sub-$70 Brent is over.

Full Goldman report here: https://t.co/sKcOx5cgj9

Global #Brokerages – Shift in Stance on Indian Equities (March–April 2026)

🔺 Goldman Sachs – Downgraded to Marketweight (from Overweight). Cut 12-month Nifty target to ~25,300 (from 29,500). Lowered earnings growth forecasts citing high crude prices, weaker GDP outlook, and risk of further downgrades.

🔺 Nomura – Downgraded to Neutral (from Overweight). December 2026 Nifty target reduced to ~24,500–24,900 (from 29,300). Advises shifting allocations toward Korea & China. Concerns: oil shocks, limited AI exposure, stretched valuations, slower domestic inflows.

🔺 Morgan Stanley – Downgraded to Equal-Weight (from Overweight in Asia/EM). Drivers: macro uncertainty, geopolitical risks, India’s vulnerability as a net energy importer.

🔺 Citi (Citigroup) – Cut stance to Neutral; trimmed targets citing Middle East conflict and crude-driven earnings risks.

🔺 Bernstein – Downgraded to Neutral; highlights inflation and earnings risks.

🔺 HSBC – Downgraded to Underweight (from Neutral). Warns rising crude could dent corporate earnings.

🔺 UBS – Downgraded to Neutral, joining broader cautious chorus.

📌 Key Takeaway:

Global brokerages have shifted from bullish/overweight to neutral/cautious on Indian equities. The common themes are:

Elevated crude oil prices from US–Iran conflict.

Macro/geopolitical risks (Strait of Hormuz disruptions).

Earnings downgrades and weaker GDP outlook.

Valuation stretch vs. peers, limited AI exposure.

Consensus: India remains structurally strong long-term, but near-term risks have prompted a reset in targets and ratings.
